contentBody: What is regularization in the context of linear regression?
option.2: Adding a penalty during training to discourage excessively large coefficients and reduce overfitting
option.3: Increasing the training set size by duplicating rows
option.4: Using only the intercept term and ignoring all features
answerExplanation: Regularization penalizes large weights so the model generalizes better instead of memorizing noise.

contentBody: Which statement best distinguishes Ridge from Lasso?
option.1: Ridge's penalty is tied to the sum of absolute coefficient values, while Lasso's penalty is tied to the sum of squared coefficient values
option.2: Ridge shrinks coefficients but typically does not set them to exactly zero; Lasso can set some coefficients exactly to zero
option.3: Ridge removes all features from the dataset before training; Lasso keeps all features
option.4: Neither Ridge nor Lasso uses a tuning setting such as alpha
answerExplanation: Ridge (L2) shrinks all coefficients; Lasso (L1) can produce exact zeros and thus feature elimination.

contentBody: Select all statements that are true about Ridge regression.
option.1: It adds a penalty related to the sum of squared coefficients
option.2: It can eliminate a feature by setting its coefficient exactly to zero
option.3: Increasing alpha generally increases the strength of shrinkage
option.4: It is often appropriate when many features are weakly but genuinely related to the target
answerExplanation: Ridge does not usually zero coefficients (that is Lasso's behavior). Stronger alpha means a stronger penalty.

contentBody: For regression, MAE and RMSE are both expressed in the same units as the target. Which statement is most accurate?
option.1: RMSE treats a 10-unit error as exactly twice as bad as a 5-unit error, same as MAE
option.2: MAE is more sensitive to a few very large errors than RMSE
option.3: RMSE penalizes large errors more heavily than MAE because errors are squared before averaging
option.4: MAE cannot be used when the target is continuous
answerExplanation: Squaring in MSE (and thus RMSE) makes large mistakes dominate more than in MAE.

contentBody: Which statements about R-squared are correct?
option.1: It measures the proportion of variance in the target explained by the model
option.2: A value of 0 means the model is equivalent to always predicting the mean of the target (on the evaluation setup used)
option.4: R-squared alone does not tell you whether absolute prediction errors are acceptable for a business use case
answerExplanation: R-squared can be negative for very poor models. Use MAE/RMSE for scale of errors; R-squared for explained variation relative to a mean baseline.

contentBody: In scikit-learn, what does the alpha hyperparameter control for Ridge and Lasso?
option.1: The fraction of data used for training versus testing
option.2: The random seed used when shuffling or splitting data
option.3: How strong the regularization penalty is
option.4: Only the step size if you were using plain gradient descent by hand
answerExplanation: Higher alpha means a stronger penalty (more shrinkage in Ridge; more zeroing in Lasso).

contentBody: A marketing analyst fits ordinary least squares to predict sales from advertising spend on TV, radio, and newspaper. Empirically, newspaper spend is often a weak or irrelevant predictor, yet OLS may still estimate a nonzero coefficient on a finite training sample. Which statements are generally plausible?
option.1: OLS may assign a nonzero weight to newspaper because of sampling noise and spurious correlation in the training data
option.2: Ridge typically shrinks the newspaper coefficient relative to OLS
option.3: Lasso may set the newspaper coefficient exactly to zero, effectively excluding that channel from the model
option.4: Regularization guarantees higher test-set R-squared than OLS on every random train/test split
answerExplanation: Regularization helps but does not guarantee better test metrics in every run or split.

contentBody: You fit a regression model and inspect test-set residuals (actual minus predicted), plotted against predictions or a feature. Which situation most strongly suggests that the model may be missing structure (for example wrong functional form or missing predictors)?
option.1: Residuals look randomly scattered with no clear trend
option.2: Residuals show a clear systematic pattern (such as a curve or funnel shape)
option.3: Training accuracy is higher than test accuracy
option.4: The model uses fewer than five features
answerExplanation: Random-looking residuals are what we hope for on a good-enough model; a systematic pattern in residuals usually means the model has not captured something important.
